operating_state: Total revenue increased 3% to $16.3 billion driven by commercial aerospace aftermarket recovery offsetting defense volume declines from supply chain constraints in Land Warfare and Air Defense programs. Commercial aerospace recovery continued with increased flight hours driving narrow-body OEM volume growth, while defense backlog increased to $30 billion with $13 billion Q2 bookings including F135 production contract.
cost_structure: Cost of sales increased $201 million to $12.9 billion with gross margin improving to 21.2% from 20.3% prior year driven by higher commercial aerospace volume. Defense operating profit declined $293 million from unfavorable EAC adjustments across multiple programs reflecting supply chain constraints, mix and performance changes, and volume impacts. Russia-related charges totaled $75 million including inventory reserves and purchase obligations.
material_events: Company reversed $1.3 billion of backlog in Q1 2022 related to Russia sanctions affecting Pratt & Whitney and Collins operations. Executed $1.8 billion in share repurchases during first half, an increase from prior year, with board declaring $0.55 per share dividend. Collins disposed of non-core businesses generating charges and completed immaterial aerospace dispositions.
macro_exposures: Russia-Ukraine conflict resulted in $210 million net tax charges from sanctions and export controls including customer financing asset impairments. Middle East customer advance payments may become refundable due to regulatory approval uncertainties. Tax legislation requiring research and experimental expenditure capitalization became effective January 2022, expected to increase full-year tax payments by estimated $2 billion beginning third quarter.
forward_outlook: Management expects continued commercial aerospace recovery driven by fundamental air travel demand, though timing of full capacity restoration remains uncertain. Company maintains remaining share repurchase authority and expects to use cash on hand for increased tax payments. Capital expenditures increased primarily for Pratt & Whitney production facility investments with factoring activity increased to $1.5 billion for enhanced cash flow management.
uncertainty_profile: Pending legislative action on research expenditure capitalization requirements could materially impact cash tax obligations. Supply chain constraints create execution risk across defense programs with continued EAC adjustment volatility. Foreign customer contracts subject to U.S. government reviews create execution risk, while Turkish supplier dependencies face potential sanctions expansion affecting aerospace component sourcing.
